author: Augustine of Hippo
book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
Pelagius Posits God’s Aid Only for Our 'Capacity.'
title: Pelagius Posits God’s Aid Only for Our 'Capacity.'

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: Chapter 7 [VI.]—Pelagius Posits God’s Aid Only for Our “Capacity.”

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: Let not Pelagius, however, in this way deceive incautious and simple persons, or even himself; for after saying, “Man is therefore to be praised for his willing and doing a good work,” he added, as if by way of correcting himself, these words: “Or rather, this praise belongs to man and to God.”

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: It was not, however, that he wished to be understood as showing any deference to the sound doctrine, that it is “God which worketh in us both to will and to do,” that he thus expressed himself; but it is clear enough, on his own showing, why he added the latter clause, for he immediately subjoins: “Who has bestowed on him the ‘capacity’ for this very will and work.”

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: From his preceding words it is manifest that he places this capacity in our nature.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: Lest he should seem, however, to have said nothing about grace, he added these words: “And who evermore, by the help of His grace, assists this very capacity,”—“this very capacity,” observe; not “very will,” or “very action;” for if he had said so much as this, he would clearly not be at variance with the teaching of the apostle.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: But there are his words: “this very capacity;” meaning that very one of the three faculties which he had placed in our nature.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: This God “evermore assists by the help of His grace.”

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: The result, indeed, is, that “the praise does not belong to man and to God,” because man so wills that yet God also inspires his volition with the ardour of love, or that man so works that God nevertheless also cooperates with him,—and without His help, what is man?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: But he has associated God in this praise in this wise, that were it not for the nature which God gave us in our creation wherewith we might be able to exercise volition and action, we should neither will nor act.
